content.text: Architecture drift in a Claude integration happens gradually: an engineer adds an inline prompt to a utility file instead of the prompts/ registry; another uses a new model ID directly in a handler without updating the approved model list; a third constructs an Anthropic client outside the shared client factory. None of these changes causes an immediate test failure. Over time they collectively undermine the structural guarantees that the CI pipeline was designed to enforce. Drift detection closes this gap by continuously comparing the live codebase against a stored architecture baseline and alerting when any departure from the approved architecture is found.

attrs.alt: Diagram: architecture drift detection pipeline from baseline snapshot through live scanner, differ, drift gate, and alert/update

content.text: What Architecture Drift Means for Claude Integrations
A Claude integration has a small set of architectural invariants that must hold everywhere in the codebase at all times:
Model IDs come from the approved list:
only Claude models that have been security-reviewed, rate-limit-allocated, and cost-budgeted are permitted. An unapproved model ID in production bypasses all of these controls.

all system prompts must be in the prompts/ directory under version control and CODEOWNERS review. An inline prompt embedded in application code cannot be reviewed, versioned, or covered by CI eval gates.
the shared client factory applies retry logic, rate-limit headers, and cost telemetry uniformly. A direct anthropic.Anthropic() construction outside the factory bypasses all of these.
every template must reference a non-deprecated safety_baseline_version. Drift to an outdated version means safety checks are weaker than the team believes.

Architecture drift is not about code quality; it is about control. Each drift category represents a case where an engineer operated outside the controlled structure — not necessarily maliciously, but with the same consequence: a system that is harder to audit, cheaper to abuse, and more expensive to fix when something goes wrong.

The baseline is a JSON file checked into the repository root that records the current approved architecture state. The drift detector compares the live codebase against this file on every CI run. When an architectural change is intentional (adding a new approved model, adding a new registry prompt), the engineer updates the baseline in the same PR — making the change visible in code review rather than silent.
The drift detector scans the live codebase to extract the current architecture state and then diffs it against the baseline. Each drift category is a separate scan function:
Blocking unapproved drift in pull requests

# .github/workflows/architecture-drift.yml
name: Architecture Drift Detection

on:
  push:
    branches: [main]
  pull_request:
    paths:
      - 'src/**'
      - 'app/**'
      - 'integrations/**'
      - 'prompts/**'

jobs:
  detect-drift:
    runs-on: ubuntu-latest
    steps:
      - uses: actions/checkout@v4

      - uses: actions/setup-python@v5
        with:
          python-version: '3.12'

      - run: pip install pyyaml

      - name: Run architecture drift detector
        id: drift
        run: python scripts/detect-architecture-drift.py 2>&1 | tee drift-report.json

      - name: Upload drift report
        if: failure()
        uses: actions/upload-artifact@v4
        with:
          name: drift-report
          path: drift-report.json

      - name: Comment drift on PR
        if: failure() && github.event_name == 'pull_request'
        uses: actions/github-script@v7
        with:
          script: |
            const fs = require('fs');
            let report = 'Architecture drift detected — see CI logs for details.';
            try {
              const raw = fs.readFileSync('drift-report.json', 'utf8');
              const data = JSON.parse(raw);
              report = '## Architecture Drift Detected\n\n```json\n'
                + JSON.stringify(data.drift, null, 2)
                + '\n```\n\nTo resolve: update `architecture.json` in this PR if the '
                + 'change is intentional, or revert the code change.';
            } catch (e) {}
            github.rest.issues.createComment({
              owner: context.repo.owner,
              repo: context.repo.repo,
              issue_number: context.issue.number,
              body: report,
            });
Updating the Baseline When Drift Is Intentional

When a team deliberately makes an architectural change — adding a new approved model, promoting a new safety baseline version — the change must go through the baseline update process rather than bypassing the drift gate:
The engineer makes the code change (e.g., adds claude-opus-4-8 as an approved model in a new template).
The drift detector runs and flags the unapproved model as drift.
The engineer updates architecture.json in the same PR: adds the new model to approved_models, updates snapshot_date.
The PR description explains why the architectural change is intentional.
The CODEOWNERS file ensures that changes to architecture.json require an architecture reviewer (prompt lead or tech lead) in addition to the regular code reviewer.
After merge, the baseline reflects the new approved state and the drift detector is clean.
Template file added outside prompts/ dir
Approved model removed from list without PR
Scheduled Drift Scan on Main Branch

The PR gate catches drift in new code. A scheduled scan on the main branch catches drift that may have existed before the gate was introduced, or drift that entered through a merge that bypassed the check:

# .github/workflows/scheduled-drift-scan.yml
name: Scheduled Architecture Drift Scan

on:
  schedule:
    - cron: '0 6 * * 1-5'   # 6am UTC, weekdays
  workflow_dispatch:         # allow manual triggers

jobs:
  scheduled-drift:
    runs-on: ubuntu-latest
    steps:
      - uses: actions/checkout@v4
        with:
          ref: main

      - uses: actions/setup-python@v5
        with:
          python-version: '3.12'

      - run: pip install pyyaml

      - name: Run drift scan on main
        run: python scripts/detect-architecture-drift.py > drift-report.json || true

      - name: Alert if drift found on main
        run: |
          STATUS=$(python -c "import json,sys; d=json.load(open('drift-report.json')); sys.exit(0 if d.get('status')=='clean' else 1)")
          if [ $? -ne 0 ]; then
            echo "DRIFT FOUND ON MAIN BRANCH — escalating to #prompt-oncall"
            # Send Slack notification via webhook
            curl -s -X POST "$SLACK_WEBHOOK_URL" \
              -H 'Content-type: application/json' \
              --data "{"text": "Architecture drift detected on main branch. See https://github.com/$GITHUB_REPOSITORY/actions/runs/$GITHUB_RUN_ID"}"
          fi
        env:
          SLACK_WEBHOOK_URL: ${{ secrets.DRIFT_ALERT_SLACK_WEBHOOK }}

When the architecture drift gate is first introduced to an existing codebase, the team cannot immediately set a clean baseline — there will almost certainly be existing violations. The setup process is:
Run the drift detector in discovery mode (without a baseline) to enumerate the current state.
Triage the findings: intentional patterns go into the approved baseline; actual violations go into a remediation backlog.
Generate the initial architecture.json from the approved patterns.
Enable the gate on new PRs with the initial baseline. The backlog of existing violations is tracked separately and fixed over the following sprints.

# scripts/generate-baseline-snapshot.py
# Run once to generate an initial architecture.json from the current codebase.
# Review the output carefully — do not blindly approve all findings.
import ast, json, pathlib, yaml

SRC_DIRS = [pathlib.Path("src"), pathlib.Path("app"), pathlib.Path("integrations")]
PROMPTS_DIR = pathlib.Path("prompts")

# Collect all model IDs currently in use
model_ids = set()
for d in SRC_DIRS:
    if d.exists():
        for path in d.rglob("*.py"):
            tree = ast.parse(path.read_text())
            for node in ast.walk(tree):
                if isinstance(node, ast.Constant) and isinstance(node.value, str):
                    v = node.value
                    if v.startswith("claude-") and len(v) > 8:
                        model_ids.add(v)

# Collect all template paths
template_paths = [str(p) for p in PROMPTS_DIR.rglob("*.yml")] if PROMPTS_DIR.exists() else []

# Collect safety baseline versions
baseline_versions = set()
for p in PROMPTS_DIR.rglob("*.yml"):
    with open(p) as f:
        t = yaml.safe_load(f)
    v = t.get("safety_baseline_version", "MISSING")
    baseline_versions.add(v)

snapshot = {
    "schema_version": "1.0",
    "snapshot_date": "FILL-IN-TODAY",
    "approved_models": sorted(model_ids),   # REVIEW: remove unapproved models
    "prompt_registry_dirs": ["prompts/"],
    "client_factory_modules": [],           # FILL IN: path(s) to your factory
    "safety_baseline_current_version": "",  # FILL IN: current canonical version
    "expected_templates": template_paths,
    "_discovery_notes": {
        "safety_baseline_versions_found": sorted(baseline_versions),
    }
}

print(json.dumps(snapshot, indent=2))

Architecture drift detection tracks the gap between the architecture that was approved and the architecture that is actually running. It works by storing a machine-readable baseline of approved architectural invariants — approved model IDs, prompt registry location, client factory modules, safety baseline version — and comparing the live codebase against that baseline on every PR and on a scheduled basis. Unapproved drift blocks CI; intentional changes require a baseline update reviewed by the architecture owners.
The baseline file (architecture.json) is the source of truth — it is updated in PRs alongside the code change, making architectural decisions visible in review.
The drift detector uses the same AST-based scanning techniques as the fitness functions, and runs in the same CI pipeline.
Drift is categorised by severity: safety baseline violations escalate to the security team; other violations block CI but do not escalate.
Scheduled scans on main catch drift that predates the gate or entered through a bypass.
When introducing the gate to an existing codebase, triage findings into the baseline (approved) vs a remediation backlog (actual violations).
